text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Chen Yiwen's gold on Friday kept up China's 100 per cent record at the Paris Games with one event to go

PARIS —Chinaare about to be crowned officially as the greatest diving nation of all time.

Chen Yiwen converting her two world titles into the individualOlympictitle on Friday meant the Chinese team has won seven gold medals for the third Games in a row.

If they win the men’s 10m platform on Saturday – as they have at the last two Olympics – it will constitute a historic clean sweep of gold medals. Eight is a lucky number in Chinese culture, but there is nothing lucky about these eight golds, which will take China clear as diving’s most successful nation with their 50th title, ahead of the Americans on 49.

China topped the diving medal table for the first time in 1988, a spot occupied by the United States almost exclusively since the First World War. The Chinese have done so ever since those Seoul Games.

So the question is, how do they do it?

“Good question!” says Britain’s Jack Laugher, second only toTom Daleyin terms ofBritish diving success.

“They are the best. It’s their national sport. They have a tremendous amount of investment. In one single city, they’ll have three, four or five different diving pools.

“I was extremely lucky that I was born where I was born, and I had a diving pool within half an hour of me, and it was a grassroots pool. It’s a small club, Harrogate Hydro, and without them, I wouldn’t be here. But for Chinese athletes potentially coming through their system, they’ve got a very streamlined way of doing it, and they’ve got a lot of people to pick from.”

Investment in sport has been a focus of the ruling Chinese Communist Party (CCP) for decades, with funding doubling every five years between 1976 and the 2008 Beijing Olympics.

Hosting the Games was no doubt a driver to upscale that investment further, and China topped the medal table with 48 golds of their 100 medals that year. Seven of those golds came in diving, as in fact 18 per cent of their all-time golds have – 49 out of 265 – but they fell one short of the clean sweep again.

“I would have loved to have trained with them at one point, and been able to actually go out to China and dive alongside them,” Laugher added. “They are my inspiration.”

But would Laugher actually have wanted to spend time one of the residential diving centres in China? Exact details are scarce, but the testimony of those who have been there suggests they are hardly holiday camps. Often, divers move to live at a specialist sports school from the age of 14.

“They don’t do much education, they are very focused on their sport,” said diver Eden Cheng, who was born in the UK to parents who emigrated from China.

“They do live at the pool. I’ve been to the national pool that one of the [Chinese] girls dives at and I’ve seen the facilities and what their life is like.

“It’s nothing compared to what we do in Britain. They are definitely next level.”

In a rare outburst at the Tokyo Games, Xie Siyi revealed how his life was “very boring”.

He added: “Every day we go from the dormitory to the training centre, then we go back to eat, then we go back to training, then we go back to sleep again.”

To an extent, it is deliberately boring. When Britain’s tennis authorities built their new National Tennis Centre in Roehampton, the rooms that athletes can stay while training were designed to be deliberately spartan and unluxurious. So the concept is not new.

But they did not happen upon the idea of scooping up athletes from all over a vast country like China at a young age and putting them in competition with each other in hotbeds of talent and competition.

Recognising that China’s huge agrarian population is an untapped resource of potential sporting ability, talent ID programmes scout rural areas for potential athletes and offer them the chance to live, train and be educated – although as Cheng points out, that is tertiary – in one of the metropolitan centres.

These are often in the south-eastern parts of the country like Guangdong, the province that produced Quan Hongchan.

In her 10m platform final, Quan produced a perfect dive that received 10s across the board from the judges. In Tokyo three years ago, she did so three times in her five dives.

Perfection is a tough standard, but Chinese divers seem to have managed it.

“There’s definitely a lot [to learn]. They are incredible with form,” says Jordan Houlden, the Brit who finished fifth in the 3m springboard Olympic final won by Xie Siyi ahead of silver medallist Wang Zongyuan.

“If you look at their arms, they’re always up by their ears when they take off. So it’s incredible watching them dive. It’s insane.

“Even for like a prelim or a semi, they can just do those dives no matter what, even if they’re the hardest dives in the world. They can do them with perfection.”

But the quest for perfection also comes with pressure, not that you would know it.

“The pressure is something that every athlete needs to overcome,” said Xie after defending his Olympic title.

“And to overcome this pressure to demonstrate our best performance in the final I would rather see the pressure as motivation.”

Their competition seem not to get disheartened by the utter dominance, despite apparently only being able to battle for bronze.

“Those athletes are incredible divers, the best of the best,” said Osmar Olvera, the Mexican who was the “best of the rest” and claimed bronze in the 3m springboard final in Paris.

“Xie in my opinion is an incredible athlete. He has inspired me, he has motivated me. Both of them have.

“I think they are the reasons why I keep improving and why I keep working hard.”

Laugher too.

“I have beaten them a few times before, and that always feels good. It’s always been my goal to try and be as good or better than them,” added Laugher, one of the only non-Chinese athletes to claim a diving gold in the last 10 years.

“Obviously they’re really strong, but I compete against them all the time. It’s a bit of an inspiration.”

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: China have won every diving gold at Paris 2024 - here's why they're so good
author: James Gray
description: Chen Yiwen's gold on Friday kept up China's 100 per cent record at the Paris Games with one event to go
